text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hey, no worries at all.
How are you doing?
Good, how are you?
I'm wonderful.
Did you have a good weekend?
I did.
It is quite warm in New England.
Where are you based?
Nice.
Where in New England are you?
I'm in Rhode Island.
Oh, sweet.
I'm out in Denver now.
I grew up on the East Coast, though, in the D.C.
area, but my dad's from New Hampshire, so used to, like, head up to the New England area all the time.
Yeah, awesome.
Awesome.
I went to school in D.C., so I'm very familiar with that.
Georgetown?
Yeah.
Yep.
So anyone who says they went to school in D.C., it's always either, it's like, I would say 80% of the time, it's Georgetown over American.
But it's so funny because American's a bigger school.

Yep, but it's one of those things where I'm like, anybody that I've ever bumped into in, like, my professional career, whenever they're like, oh, I went to school in D.C., I'm like, it's always Georgetown.
Nice.
That's awesome.
Interesting.
Yeah.
Well, since I joined late, we'll jump in.
I will give you, like, pretty standard interview format.
I'll give you a sense of how I'm thinking about the role, how it fits within GrowthX, we can talk about you, and then I'll try and leave time at the end for you to ask me questions.
Um, but yeah, I am the technical managing editor is works within the pods in our delivery area.
So growthx, like really at its heart is a like product.
It's a product built on a marketing agency, or that's like a product that is the basis for a marketing agency.
Um, so we are really content, like blog content, um, content only.
So like no socials, like we're not creating videos for clients really.
Like, you know, we have that on our own growth, but not, uh, it's not part of what we're doing for clients.
Um, and our content is generated by our own internal platform called Atlas.
Um, have you chatted with Panzer already?
Yes.
Yeah.
Um, yeah, so you probably know.

More about Atlas, won't spend a lot of time talking about that, but essentially, like, this person is working with the content managers who are working in Atlas, and there are a couple of aspects that are going to make this role successful.
So, one is, like, editorial taste and, like, the ability to edit.

So, the content managers generating content, they're doing a first pass, you're responsible, you're, like, the owner for what goes out, like, you, it's your name kind of on the, on what we're delivering.
You're also responsible for, like, day-to-day or, like, our deliverables week over week, and making sure that we're, like, on track for meeting contract, contractual obligations for a client.
Yep.
And then, the other piece is you're working with the engineering.

Team like you have to know what makes content good as well working with the engineering team on helping improve kind of what goes in what are the prompts like where is the pipeline going wrong things like that on like, the the editorial front.
So communicating that back to our engineering team to make the product better.
You'll be working with a account strategist.
So managing editors, three to five accounts, account strategists, have like two to three pods of managing editors.
And they're kind of like thinking long term about the relationship and like, kind of the strategic direction of what we're generating for the client.
So you're like, very much the day to day project manager and client owner in a lot of ways.
You have somebody supporting you on the content side and then somebody thinking.
Kind of high level to support that.
Any questions?
No, yeah, that all makes total sense.
Cool, yeah.
If you chatted with Panzer, I'm guessing that was a little bit repetitive.
So let's talk about you.
Yeah, give me a little bit of your background and your elevator pitch.
Yeah, I'll run you through it.
So like I said, I grew up in the DC area.
So fresh out of school, I got involved in kind of the DC startup scene.
Helped kind of launch an e-com brand, which then ended up being an introduction into an SEO firm out there.
At the SEO firm, I was like pretty early on, I think they were about a year-ish in.
So big SEO startup was running.

So probably like just over maybe 15-ish accounts at the like peak of my time there at one time, not in the total span, but like 15-ish accounts at one moment with some of the bigger clients we had there, which were primarily lawyers and some tech businesses.
So did that, got super involved in kind of building out their documentation since I was an early hire.
I like really geek out on like systems and kind of building out guides.
So I did a bunch of that for them, ended up training all of their staff and writing all of their scripts and the templates that they would use and things like that.
Had a good time there.
But ended up kind of switching gears and moving over to an architecture firm that wanted to do a rebrand.
And they called themselves a startup, but they were like 20 years in.
So still a small scrappy team, but kind of like in that more later stages of things, right?
Led their rebrand, redesigned their website and helped them launch a sub-brand.
And while I did that, I also won them five international design awards with some write-ups that I was writing for them.
I used that five write-ups as kind of my content portfolio because I was also just freelance writing on the side.
And that ended up starting my journalism career, but also it's not really journalism.
It's affiliate marketing and it's growth marketing kind
So I was the third editor to sign on at US News and World Report to build out 360 reviews.
Built a bunch of their categories, but again, I just geek out on the kind of system side of things.
So I built out all the documentation there, the guides, the tone guides, the style guides, the process guides, everything.
So after launching a bunch of categories, they tried to make me senior editor.
And within a month, they changed their mind and said, hey, actually, can you be the content operations manager and just teach everybody what you're doing?
Because your categories are performing really well.
So I did that, kind of became that like cross department guy of, hey, let's smooth out all of these systems and let's make things with the...
Data team and the editorial team smoother and make this all more efficient.
That was going exceptionally well, but my manager had actually moved over to Forbes at the time.
And Forbes was creating a secret little partnership with USA Today.
And she told them, hey, if you want a guy to come in here and launch this for you, Bryce is your guy.
So I was the first editor to sign on to USA Today Blueprint.
I launched that for them, built out mainly their small business vertical, which is kind of before I did my content operation stuff, I kind of moved into the small business niche over at US News.
So built out the small business vertical for USA Today Blueprint, it was going exceptionally well.
Uh...
Uh...
Making like 70% of total revenue, which was awesome.
Had their highest performing pages and was just killing it in the SERPs.
And then at that point, Forbes, their small business team had started to kind of underperform.
And they did a major layoff.
They basically wiped out that entire team, left one editor on the team.
And they asked me to move over to the Forbes side of things and leave USA Today to my associate editor.
So I left that to her.
She took that over and I moved over to the Forbes website.
And I rebuilt Forbes small business from scratch.
And now it's the highest performing vertical there.
It makes over 50.
50% of total revenue for Forbes, which is...
Help me understand what the small business vertical means at a publication.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
So basically, any content that would be covering small business, that is what falls under that vertical.
So I guess easiest way to kind of conceptualize that is obviously Forbes is huge, right?
So anything that's going to have that, like, slash small business after the Forbes.com, that's going to be coming from me, right?
So it's essentially like the category of content.
Does that make sense?
Yeah, and in terms of revenue, so the thing I wasn't sure about was it like you guys were writing, like, advertorial copy, copy or, like, what side of that.
What was it?
So when you say revenue, that's just like through the like quick ads and stuff that you run on those sites?
Ads and then a lot of the content is affiliate marketing.
So basically it's that kind of like we're doing everything from top of funnel where it's like how to file for an LLC in Colorado or Rhode Island or whatever, right?
And then that moves you through the funnel and eventually you get to a review of our partner, Inc.
File, who if you click on the link at some point on the page, it brings you over to their site and we get a little portion of the money.
feel like backlinking and stuff like that.
So that as well as like, I'm sure you've seen, there's like a million of them for everything now, but the like best lists where it's like 10 best.
Sneakers of 2025, stuff like that, you know?
So a lot of that drives a ton of the revenue for these sites.
So like SEO, basically like winning SEO categories related to small business.
100%, yeah.
Okay.
So basically like even though I'm no longer like working at an SEO firm, like the majority of all of this is all based on the fact that like SEO has been the thing that drives the most revenue.
So it's more of those articles that can kind of nail the SEO side of things and less of those articles where it's like, here's what happened in today's news.
Yeah, yeah.
Okay, helpful.
So like you talked, you've been in-house at some places, you've built some systems.
I'm really curious about like when you were supporting 15 accounts or if you have an example of like supporting.
multiple accounts, and thinking about week-on-week deliverables, and meeting deadlines like that, and yeah.
Yeah, yeah, absolutely.
Even with the, like, managing editor roles, and kind of at the publications, like, a lot of it, more so is project management than anything.
It's like, can you run a tight content plan, and make sure that we publish this much this week, and don't miss the mark, right?
So a lot of it is that, but when it came to running accounts, and then kind of, now that I'm, like, more in-house, I also do, like, consulting on the side.
Yeah, well, talk to me about generating, like you said, tight content plan.
What does that mean to you?
Oh, yeah.
So for that, like, I think kind of content plans are where a lot of people end up messing up the most, in at least publications.
With, with the way I kind of see things, like, I think.
A content plan should have everything, like, laid out, like, down to the, like, tiniest detail.
So I will aggressively plan at least the month if, like, I try and plan three quarter, or, like, or three months at a time, the whole quarter.
And I will, like, make a list of every single piece of content that is going to be published over that quarter.
And then who is assigned to it, the due date for writing, the due date for editing, the due date for second edits, the due date that it's going to go live on the website.
like, think that the more meticulous that you are when it comes to the initial planning phase, the easier it is to make sure that you never miss a mark.
Because then it's, like, oh, hey, Bob, Lisa, whoever, right?
Like, it is X date, this stage is supposed to be done, like, where are we at?
And generally, you're working off of, like...
Airtable or a Kanban board or whatever system.
So you can kind of make sure like it's moving according to your content plan.

But more so in general, just like if you nail all of that detail from the start and like set those very clear expectations with the people that work like with you, everyone can kind of be on board really easy.
Um, as well as like, I do a lot of check-in calls where I like make my team meet with me, um, Monday so that I can kind of make sure we know the plan for the week.
Everybody knows exactly what they need to deliver.
Um, and then kind of make sure that any cross department kind of stuff, um, there's a weekly meeting as well, just to kind of make sure everybody's on the same page and knows what's going on.
Yeah.
Um, so as a planner, you also know that things change.
So talk to me about a time when you had to like quickly.
If strategy for a client, something that had to happen, yeah.
Yeah, I mean, that is kind of also just the most common thing to occur, especially when Google has, like, updated the algorithm.
I don't even know how many times at this point this year.

But I think one of the best examples, which is, like, one of the most chaotic, probably, moments of my career, was last November, Forbes, Google did a manual action against everyone where they took a bunch of big players off of Google's SERPs because they saw that, basically, people were expanding beyond, like, the scope of what their website was covering, right?
So, like, for...
For instance, a bunch of Forbes verticals got taken out in the manual action because we were covering like best CBD gummies.
And it's like that has nothing to do with Forbes.
So in that moment, it was a big switch because, right, it's November.
We're headed towards December.
We're shut down for the last two weeks of December for the holidays.
So it's kind of that period where everyone's trying to get ahead.
But now we have to really switch gears and figure out what to do because boom, all our content just got pulled off.
And in those moments, totally normal for people to freak out and worry.
But like I was like within less than an hour of all that happening, like in a meeting with the VP of content talking to her.
I was like, OK, like, what's the plan?
How are we going to do this?
Like, let's figure this out immediately after meeting.

And with my team, talking to them about the plan that I wanted to put in place, and I led a major overhaul of like, let's redo all of our content templates, let's focus in on the 100 most important pages, and let's do a big push.
And then we got all of that kind of knocked out as fast as humanly possible.

We're able to resubmit a handful of pages to Google and say like, hey, we are taking into account some of the things you said, we have drastically overhauled our templates, we're doing all of these things, and basically submitting like a reassessment proposal, and Google accepted it.
And we ended up getting put back on Google, kind of because of the little spearhead march that I did there, which was huge.
So I think in those moments, the key
You have like, there's always going to be something that goes wrong.
And like, that's why it's so important to have such a strong plan from the start.

Because if you have such a strong plan at the start, the moment that things become chaos, it's easier to switch gears, because it's, it's harder to switch gears if you already are living in chaos of like not running a smooth plan.

And it's easier to drop everything if you are running a smooth plan, because everybody knows where everything is at, you can just drop it and move on to like, hey, new priority, this is more important.
While you guys are working on that, I'll redo the plan for you.
And I'll have you a new plan today to show you what the updated version looks like.
Yeah, awesome.
Okay, last question is around like your AI experience.
So how are you integrating like AI into the work that you do?
Yeah.
Um, so
So AI at Forbes is quite difficult.
There's a lot of people who are super hesitant about AI.
And I always make the joke, like I grew up watching like Terminator and the Matrix.
like I get it.
But I am a huge AI like advocate.
So I'm on the like AI board at Forbes, trying to convince them to basically incorporate it.
But we use it on my team.
My team though also like obviously has that kind of, it's easier to use it for us because we're already reviewing things like Zapier.
We're already reviewing HubSpot and like Airtable and all of the kind of platforms that are more mainstream that are working automations and AI workflows into things.
So I think it's a little easier to naturally have like a team of people who are more interested in it.
But, um, Forbes is not as excited about it.

Um, so, I also, like I said, do some consulting, um, and outside of obviously, like, convincing my team to use it, but, and trying to convince Forbes to use it in general, um, I do some consulting and, uh, I'm kind of leading some, uh, uh, content initiative for this thing called the AI to SMB, um, Center of Excellence.
It's super wordy.

I did not choose the name, uh, but, uh, there's, uh, there's a guy, Dave, and he runs, uh, B to SMB, which is this kind of, um, executive brain trust type group where it's a bunch of, um, kind of tech leaders and executives that all get together and talk about go to market strategy and whatnot.
Um, uh,
And how to sell to small businesses.
So they're starting to understand AI is going to be super important.
And I've been kind of helping him shape like, okay, like, this is how we can talk about it on the executive level.
Well, at the same time, they don't know what to do.
And I'm like, everyone's fear is like, you'll just fire the whole company and bring AI in.
So I've been kind of helping in that aspect and like, showing them like, this is what an AI workflow looks like.
This is how to write a good AI prompt and things like that.
And did a nice little presentation for some cool people.
was like, executives from Google and Apple and Microsoft and whatnot that I got to talk to about it.
And I did a podcast, basically just preaching the AI gospel, which that's kind of where I'm at.
Like, I am super pro-AI.
Forbes is not as much pro-AI, which is why I am finding myself kind of trying to move into your side of things.
Fair enough.
Well, I do have a hard stop in three minutes, and I want to make sure there's time for you to ask me questions or a question.
So what can I answer?
Um, my go-to question, since we only have a little bit of time, I always just like to hear what's your favorite thing and your least favorite thing about working at GrowthX.
I think that's especially interesting when it is such a young startup.
So that would be my question for you.
Yeah, uh, favorite thing is I think that the business model is really interesting and exciting.
Um, and I, like, it's just...
It's not SaaS, which is kind of nice as someone who's worked in tech and SaaS for a while.
So I think that's cool.
I think the business model makes a lot of sense and gives us some breathing room to really think about how we want to run operations.
I think we are so popular that my least favorite thing is that it is a scramble.
So you have to be really, really scrappy, and there are going to be extra hours, and it's sometimes messy.
You're catching me on a day when it's been messier than usual, but that is startup life.
And it's because everybody's so excited to work with us.
So, yeah.
Yeah, I mean, you guys are rapidly growing.
It's insane.
Yeah.
Okay.
Well, yeah, we have a hard stop, so I don't want to take any more of your time, but thank you so much.
This has been awesome.
I really enjoyed meeting you.
All right.
You too.
Thanks, Bryce.
Yeah.
Have a great day.
Hope it gets a little better.
It's almost over.
Thanks.
